structured_choices.data_source: Retrospective cohort of 50,000 patients from a single healthcare system's EHR with linked genomic sequencing results, spanning 5 years (Jan 2019–Dec 2023). Data includes all inpatient discharges with complete EHR records and genomic data availability. Genomic sequencing availability is treated as an inclusion criterion, not missing-data imputation.
structured_choices.label_definition: Binary readmission outcome: patient readmitted to any acute care bed within 30 calendar days of index discharge. Readmission is defined using standardized administrative claims data (readmission flag in discharge summary or new admission record within 30-day window). Unplanned readmissions only; planned procedures excluded via procedure code review.
structured_choices.split_method: Temporal stratified split. Data is divided chronologically: Jan 2019–Jun 2021 (70%, ~35k patients) for training, Jul 2021–Dec 2021 (15%, ~7.5k patients) for validation, Jan 2022–Dec 2023 (15%, ~7.5k patients) for held-out test. Within each split, stratification by hospital site and baseline readmission risk quartile (computed from demographics + prior utilization only, on training data) to ensure each fold is representative of case-mix variation across institutions. Temporal split prevents data leakage and accounts for secular trends in clinical practice and sequencing methods. Site stratification controls for institutional policy differences.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformers (scaling, categorical encoding, missingness flags, clinical note vectorization) are fit exclusively on the training set (Jan 2019–Jun 2021). These fitted transformers are then applied identically to validation and test sets without refitting. Genomic feature engineering (e.g., rare variant aggregation, pathway-level summaries) is fit on training set genomic data only. This prevents validation/test data statistics from influencing preprocessing and avoids leakage.
structured_choices.features: Three feature groups, constructed separately then concatenated: (1) Demographic/administrative: age, sex, insurance type, hospital site, admission source, primary diagnosis (ICD-10 mapped to clinically-defined risk groups). (2) Clinical EHR features: comorbidity index (Charlson), prior 12-month utilization (ED visits, inpatient days, readmissions), primary diagnosis severity, medication count at discharge, lab values in final 7 days pre-discharge (eGFR, hemoglobin, glucose, albumin—median-imputed if missing, with missingness flags retained). Clinical notes (discharge summaries) are vectorized via TF-IDF (vocabulary fit on training notes only) or pre-trained clinical BERT embeddings (frozen, not fine-tuned to preserve external validity). (3) Genomic features: rare variant burden (aggregated by OMIM pathway and disease relevance to readmission risk factors like cardiovascular, renal, immune conditions), common variant polygenic risk scores (PRS) for relevant phenotypes (pre-computed, not re-derived), copy-number variations, and a 'sequencing quality flag' (depth, coverage, call rate). All genomic features are z-score normalized within training set only. No feature is constructed using the readmission label or post-discharge information.
structured_choices.model: Multi-modal deep learning architecture: (a) EHR/demographic tabular branch: 2–3 fully connected layers (128–256 units, ReLU, 20% dropout). (b) Clinical note embedding branch (BERT or TF-IDF): single dense layer (128 units, ReLU). (c) Genomic branch: 2–3 fully connected layers (64–128 units, ReLU, 20% dropout). Branches are concatenated and passed through 2–3 final dense layers (128–256 units, ReLU, 20% dropout) before sigmoid output. Architecture is regularized via L2 (λ=0.001) and dropout to prevent overfitting on the relatively small genomic feature set. Model is trained via Adam optimizer, binary cross-entropy loss, early stopping on validation AUC (patience=10 epochs).
structured_choices.baseline: Logistic regression (L2 regularization, C=1.0) trained on the same demographic and clinical EHR features (tabular only, no clinical notes or genomic data). The baseline receives equivalent hyperparameter tuning: L2 penalty parameter C is selected via grid search over {0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1, 10} on validation data using the same primary metric (AUC). Both baseline and proposed model use identical feature preprocessing, identical train/val/test splits, and identical evaluation conditions. This ensures the comparison isolates the contribution of multi-modal integration and deep learning architecture, not confounding factors like data handling or tuning effort.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC-ROC) on the held-out test set. Justification: (1) The operational goal is to identify high-risk readmission patients for targeted interventions; AUC-ROC measures discrimination across all decision thresholds, which is appropriate when the optimal threshold has not yet been set operationally. (2) The baseline readmission rate is expected to be ~15–25% (typical for acute admissions); AUC-ROC is robust to class imbalance and does not assume a fixed threshold. (3) Clinical stakeholders can later select a threshold (sensitivity vs. specificity trade-off) based on intervention capacity and resource constraints; AUC provides the foundation for this decision. (4) AUC-ROC is standard for model comparison in clinical ML and facilitates interpretation across institutions.
secondary_metrics: Precision-recall curve and area under the PR curve (AUPRC) — more sensitive to class imbalance and directly relevant if the clinical team has a target positive predictive value or intervention budget.
Sensitivity (true positive rate) and specificity (true negative rate) at a pre-specified operating point (e.g., 80% sensitivity), to evaluate clinical utility at a fixed threshold.

Calibration: expected calibration error (ECE) on test set, using decile-wise bins. Poor calibration indicates the model's predicted probabilities do not reflect true event rates, which undermines clinical decision-making.

Per-site performance: AUC-ROC, sensitivity, specificity stratified by hospital site, to detect whether the multi-modal model generalizes across institutional variations or exhibits site-dependent bias.
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Validation set (Jul 2021–Dec 2021, ~7.5k patients). Hyperparameter tuning (network architecture, dropout rates, L2 penalty, learning rate) is performed via grid search or random search, training multiple model instances on the training set and evaluating on validation set using AUC-ROC. Early stopping within each training run also monitors validation loss. The model with the highest validation AUC-ROC is selected. The test set is not used during model selection and is evaluated exactly once at the end.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The held-out test set (Jan 2022–Dec 2023, ~7.5k patients) is reserved and not accessed until model selection is complete. After the final model is locked, all primary and secondary metrics are computed on the test set in a single evaluation run. Results are reported with 95% confidence intervals (bootstrapped over test samples) for AUC-ROC and secondary metrics. No tuning, feature engineering, or model re-selection occurs after test set evaluation.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Hospital site is treated as a stratification variable in the train/val/test split to ensure each fold contains proportional representation of institutional policies. Secondary analysis includes per-site performance evaluation; substantial site-level variation would suggest institutional confounds are influencing results and may limit generalizability. Temporal split inherently controls for secular trends in clinical practice, EHR documentation patterns, and genomic sequencing availability (which may have improved over time). In sensitivity analysis, we would also control for admission source and primary diagnosis severity to isolate the added value of genomic data conditional on clinical factors. The design does not establish causation (this is observational data); it establishes whether multi-modal integration improves discrimination. It does not establish whether improved predictions translate to reduced readmissions in practice (that requires a prospective intervention trial).

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: Multi-Modal Deep Learning for Patient Readmission Prediction

### 1. Data Source and Label Definition

We will use retrospective EHR and genomic data from approximately 50,000 patients across a single healthcare system spanning January 2019 through December 2023. The inclusion criterion is all inpatient discharges with complete EHR records and linked genomic sequencing results. The readmission outcome is defined as a binary label: patient readmitted to acute care within 30 calendar days of index discharge, excluding planned procedures. Readmission status is extracted from administrative discharge records and standardized admission records, ensuring consistent definition across all institutions within the system.

### 2. Data Splitting and Temporal Control

The data will be divided using a temporal stratified split to prevent information leakage and control for secular trends. The split is: training set (Jan 2019–Jun 2021, 70%, ~35,000 patients), validation set (Jul 2021–Dec 2021, 15%, ~7,500 patients), and held-out test set (Jan 2022–Dec 2023, 15%, ~7,500 patients). Within each split, patients are further stratified by hospital site and baseline readmission risk quartile (computed using only demographic and prior utilization features, derived from training data). This stratification ensures that each fold has proportional representation of institutional variation and case-mix, addressing the known confound that hospital policies and resources vary across sites.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Three feature groups are constructed and concatenated:

**(a) Demographic and Administrative:** Age, sex, insurance type, hospital site, admission source, and primary diagnosis (ICD-10 codes mapped to clinically-defined risk groups).

**(b) Clinical EHR Features:** Charlson comorbidity index, 12-month prior utilization (ED visits, inpatient days, prior readmissions), primary diagnosis severity, medication count at discharge, and laboratory values in the final 7 days pre-discharge (eGFR, hemoglobin, glucose, albumin). Missing lab values are imputed using median values computed on the training set, with missingness flags retained as features. Clinical discharge summaries are vectorized using either TF-IDF (vocabulary fit on training notes only) or frozen pre-trained clinical BERT embeddings to capture narrative patterns predictive of readmission.

**(c) Genomic Features:** Rare variant burden aggregated by OMIM pathway (focusing on cardiovascular, renal, and immune pathways relevant to readmission), polygenic risk scores for relevant phenotypes, copy-number variations, and sequencing quality flags (depth, coverage, call rate). All genomic features are z-score normalized using training set statistics.

All preprocessing transformers (scaling, imputation, vectorization, normalization) are fit exclusively on the training set and applied identically to validation and test sets without refitting. No feature encodes the readmission label or uses post-discharge information.

### 4. Model Architecture and Baseline

The proposed multi-modal model consists of three parallel branches: (1) tabular EHR branch with 2–3 fully connected layers (128–256 units, ReLU, 20% dropout), (2) clinical note embedding branch with single dense layer (128 units, ReLU), and (3) genomic branch with 2–3 fully connected layers (64–128 units, ReLU, 20% dropout). These branches are concatenated and passed through 2–3 final dense layers before a sigmoid output. The model is regularized via L2 (λ=0.001) and dropout and trained using Adam optimizer, binary cross-entropy loss, and early stopping on validation AUC (patience=10 epochs).

The baseline is logistic regression trained on demographic and clinical EHR features only (no clinical notes or genomic data). The baseline receives equivalent hyperparameter tuning: L2 parameter C is selected via grid search over {0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1, 10} on the validation set using the same primary metric. Both models use identical preprocessing, splits, and evaluation conditions, ensuring the comparison isolates the contribution of multi-modal integration.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is **Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUC-ROC)** on the held-out test set. AUC-ROC is appropriate because it measures discrimination across all decision thresholds, is robust to class imbalance (~15–25% baseline readmission rate), and allows clinicians to select an operating threshold based on intervention capacity. Secondary metrics include Precision-Recall AUC (more sensitive to imbalance), sensitivity and specificity at a fixed operating point (80% sensitivity), calibration (expected calibration error), and per-site AUC-ROC to detect institutional bias.

### 6. Model Selection and Test Set Policy

Hyperparameter tuning is performed exclusively on the validation set. Multiple model instances are trained on the training set, evaluated on validation set AUC-ROC, and the highest-performing instance is selected. The test set remains untouched during model development. After final model selection, all metrics are computed on the test set exactly once, with 95% confidence intervals (bootstrapped).

### 7. Controls and Scope

Site stratification in the split controls for institutional variation. Temporal split controls for secular trends in clinical practice and sequencing availability. The design establishes whether multi-modal integration improves discrimination; it does not establish causation or whether improved predictions reduce readmissions in practice (requiring a prospective trial).
